name: Entrepreneurship Fundamentals
description: An action-oriented introduction to entrepreneurship — idea generation, problem-solving, lean startup methodology, business model design, pitching, and building an entrepreneurial mindset.

modules.title: The Entrepreneurial Mindset
modules.objectives: Cultivate a growth mindset; understand failure as a learning tool; develop creativity, resilience, and the ability to recognize entrepreneurial opportunities.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Entrepreneurship — The Entrepreneurial Mindset
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — What Makes a Good Entrepreneur?
modules.video2Note: Y Combinator — How to Succeed with a Startup (Sam Altman)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Entrepreneurial Mindset Practice Questions
modules.assignment: Write a 1-page personal reflection: describe a time you failed at something and what you learned from it. Then list 3 qualities you want to strengthen as an entrepreneur and explain how you plan to develop them.

modules.title: Identifying Problems & Opportunities
modules.objectives: Apply design thinking principles; create an empathy map; identify customer pain points; recognize gaps in the market.
modules.readingNote: IDEO Design Thinking — What is Design Thinking?
modules.video2Note: Y Combinator Startup School — How to Get Startup Ideas
modules.assignment: Choose a problem you observe in your school or community. Create an empathy map for the people affected. List at least 5 specific pain points and identify 2 potential market gaps that an entrepreneur could address.

modules.title: Ideation Techniques
modules.objectives: Apply brainstorming, SCAMPER, mind mapping, and lateral thinking to generate creative business ideas.
modules.readingNote: IDEO Design Thinking — The Power of Brainstorming
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Creating an Opportunity Practice
modules.assignment: Run a timed 10-minute brainstorm on the problem from Module 2. Then apply the SCAMPER technique to your top idea. Finally, create a mind map connecting your best 5 ideas to potential customer needs.

modules.title: Customer Discovery
modules.objectives: Design and conduct customer interviews; validate assumptions; apply the jobs-to-be-done framework to understand customer motivations.
modules.videoNote: Y Combinator — How to Talk to Users (Eric Migicovsky)
modules.assignment: Write 10 open-ended customer interview questions for your target user. Interview at least 2 people and record their responses. Summarize your findings: what assumptions were validated? What was surprising? Apply the jobs-to-be-done framework to describe the core job your customer is trying to accomplish.

modules.title: The Lean Startup
modules.objectives: Understand the build-measure-learn cycle; define and build a minimum viable product (MVP); understand when and how to pivot.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Entrepreneurship — The Lean Startup
modules.videoNote: Y Combinator — How to Build an MVP (Michael Seibel)
modules.assignment: Design an MVP for your business idea. Describe: (1) the single core assumption you are testing, (2) the simplest version of your product or service that tests that assumption, (3) how you will measure success. Sketch or describe the MVP in one page.

modules.title: Business Model Canvas
modules.objectives: Identify and explain all nine building blocks of the Business Model Canvas; articulate a clear value proposition, customer segments, and revenue streams.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Entrepreneurship — Business Model Canvas
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — The Business Model Canvas Explained
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Business Model Canvas Practice
modules.assignment: Complete a full Business Model Canvas for your startup idea. Fill in all nine blocks with specific, concrete details (not generic descriptions). Write a 1-paragraph summary explaining how your value proposition connects to your customer segment and revenue streams.

modules.title: Market Research & Competitive Analysis
modules.objectives: Distinguish primary from secondary research; map the competitive landscape; identify your differentiation strategy.
modules.videoNote: Y Combinator — How to Think About Competition
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How to Do Market Research
modules.assignment: Identify 3 direct and 2 indirect competitors for your business idea. Create a competitive matrix comparing your startup against each on at least 5 criteria (price, features, target customer, etc.). Write a paragraph explaining how your startup differentiates itself.

modules.title: Value Proposition Design
modules.objectives: Craft a compelling value proposition; define your unique selling point (USP); position your product clearly in the market.
modules.readingNote: IDEO Design Thinking — Value Proposition Design
modules.videoNote: Y Combinator — How to Communicate Your Value Proposition
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — What Makes a Great Value Proposition?
modules.assignment: Write three different versions of your value proposition (one sentence each): one focused on the customer problem, one on your solution's benefits, and one on your differentiation. Test each with a classmate and refine based on their feedback. Submit your final value proposition with a brief explanation of why you chose it.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a growth mindset in entrepreneurship?
options: Believing your abilities are fixed and cannot change
Viewing failure as a permanent setback
Seeing challenges as opportunities to learn and improve
Avoiding risks to preserve existing success
questions.answer: Seeing challenges as opportunities to learn and improve
questions.explanation: A growth mindset means believing abilities can be developed through dedication and hard work, treating failures as learning experiences rather than permanent defeats.

questions.question: The first step in design thinking, as used in opportunity identification, is:
questions.explanation: Design thinking begins with empathizing — deeply understanding the people you are designing for and the problems they face before defining or solving anything.

questions.question: SCAMPER is a technique used during which phase of the entrepreneurial process?
questions.explanation: SCAMPER (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Reverse) is an ideation technique used to generate creative ideas by modifying existing concepts.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "In customer discovery, the primary purpose of interviews is to:" is: Validate or invalidate your assumptions about customer needs.
questions.explanation: True. Validate or invalidate your assumptions about customer needs is correct.

questions.question: What does MVP stand for in the lean startup methodology?
questions.explanation: An MVP (Minimum Viable Product) is the simplest version of a product that allows a team to collect validated learning about customers with the least effort.

questions.question: In the lean startup build-measure-learn cycle, what happens immediately after you BUILD your MVP?
options: You measure the results of your experiment
questions.answer: You measure the results of your experiment
questions.explanation: The build-measure-learn loop requires measuring customer response data after building the MVP before deciding to persevere or pivot.

questions.question: How many building blocks does the Business Model Canvas have?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 9.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is an example of primary market research" is: Reading an industry report published by a research firm.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Interviewing 10 potential customers about their needs.

questions.question: A competitive advantage describes:
options: How much money a startup has raised
The unique attribute that allows a business to outperform competitors
The number of employees a company has
The speed at which a company launches its product
questions.answer: The unique attribute that allows a business to outperform competitors
questions.explanation: A competitive advantage is the distinct quality (lower cost, unique feature, stronger brand, etc.) that allows a business to generate more value for customers than its rivals.

questions.question: The 'jobs-to-be-done' framework focuses on:
options: The technical specifications of a product
The underlying task or goal a customer is trying to accomplish
The number of job openings at a startup
The legal requirements for hiring employees
questions.answer: The underlying task or goal a customer is trying to accomplish
questions.explanation: Jobs-to-be-done theory argues that customers 'hire' products to help them make progress in their lives — focusing on what job they need done rather than demographics.

questions.question: Which building block of the Business Model Canvas describes the group of people or organizations a business aims to reach?
questions.explanation: Customer Segments defines the different groups of people or organizations an enterprise aims to reach and serve.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A 'pivot' in the lean startup context means:" is: Changing a fundamental element of the business strategy based on validated learning.
questions.explanation: True. Changing a fundamental element of the business strategy based on validated learning is correct.

questions.question: An empathy map typically captures which four dimensions of a user's experience?
questions.explanation: An empathy map captures what users Say, Think, Do, and Feel — helping teams build deeper empathy and gain insight into customer needs.

questions.question: Describe the difference between primary and secondary market research. Give one example of each and explain when you would use each type for a startup.
questions.answer: Primary research collects new, original data directly (e.g., customer interviews, surveys). Secondary research uses existing published data (e.g., industry reports, census data). Primary research is best when you need specific insights about your target customer's exact problems; secondary research is useful for understanding market size or broader trends without the cost of original data collection.
questions.explanation: Both types are complementary: secondary research helps frame the landscape; primary research validates specific assumptions about your customer.

questions.question: Explain the build-measure-learn cycle in your own words. Why is it important for a startup to go through this loop quickly?
questions.answer: Build-measure-learn: build the smallest possible version of your product (MVP), measure how real customers respond, and learn whether your assumptions were correct. Speed is critical because early-stage startups have limited resources; the faster they run experiments, the sooner they can validate or invalidate ideas before spending money on the wrong direction.
questions.explanation: The loop reduces waste by testing assumptions cheaply and quickly, enabling data-driven decisions rather than guesswork.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Resilience in entrepreneurship is best described as:" is: Never making mistakes.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The ability to recover quickly from setbacks and keep moving forward.

questions.question: Which design thinking phase involves generating as many ideas as possible without judgment?
questions.explanation: The Ideate phase encourages divergent thinking — generating a large volume of ideas freely before narrowing down to the best solutions.

questions.question: A startup's value proposition answers which core question?
options: How much does the product cost?
Why should a customer choose you over the alternatives?
How many employees do you have?
questions.answer: Why should a customer choose you over the alternatives?
questions.explanation: A value proposition communicates the specific benefits a product delivers and why those benefits make it the best choice for the target customer compared to alternatives.

questions.question: Which revenue model charges customers a recurring fee at regular intervals?
questions.explanation: A subscription model charges customers a recurring fee (monthly, annually) in exchange for ongoing access to a product or service.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Burn rate is defined as:" is: The rate at which a startup spends its cash reserves.
questions.explanation: True. The rate at which a startup spends its cash reserves is correct.

questions.question: The break-even point is reached when:
options: A startup raises its first round of funding
questions.explanation: Break-even is the point at which a business's total revenue exactly covers its total costs — neither making a profit nor a loss.

questions.question: Growth hacking is best described as:
options: Using creative, low-cost marketing experiments to grow a user base rapidly
Hiring a large sales team to cold-call customers
questions.answer: Using creative, low-cost marketing experiments to grow a user base rapidly
questions.explanation: Growth hacking focuses on creative, data-driven, low-budget experiments to rapidly acquire and retain users, typically used by resource-constrained startups.

questions.question: An elevator pitch should ideally be completed in:
questions.explanation: An elevator pitch is a concise, compelling summary of your business idea delivered in the time it takes to ride an elevator — typically 30 to 60 seconds.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "When splitting equity among co-founders, the most important factor to consider is:" is: Who had the original idea.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Each founder's ongoing contribution, skills, and commitment to the startup.

questions.question: Which business structure provides limited liability protection while allowing profits to pass through to owners without corporate taxation?
questions.explanation: An LLC provides limited liability (protecting personal assets) and pass-through taxation, making it a popular choice for small startups.

questions.question: A trademark protects:
options: A brand name, logo, or slogan that identifies a business
An original creative work like a book or music
questions.answer: A brand name, logo, or slogan that identifies a business
questions.explanation: Trademarks protect words, names, symbols, or logos that distinguish the source of goods or services. Patents protect inventions; copyrights protect creative works; trade secrets protect confidential business information.

questions.question: A freemium business model offers:
options: A basic version for free with premium features available for payment
A free trial period followed by mandatory subscription
Products free of charge only to non-profit customers
questions.answer: A basic version for free with premium features available for payment
questions.explanation: Freemium gives users a free tier to reduce acquisition friction while monetizing a subset who want advanced functionality.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is the best example of word-of-mouth marketing" is: A happy customer recommending your product to a friend unprompted.
questions.explanation: True. A happy customer recommending your product to a friend unprompted is correct.

questions.question: In a pitch deck, the 'Ask' slide communicates:
options: How much funding you are seeking and what you will use it for
questions.answer: How much funding you are seeking and what you will use it for
questions.explanation: The Ask slide tells investors exactly what you need (e.g., $500,000) and how you will allocate it (e.g., 60% product development, 40% marketing).

questions.question: Which of the following best represents an ethical consideration a startup should address?
options: Choosing the most profitable pricing model
Ensuring that user data is collected and used transparently with informed consent
Hiring the least expensive employees available
questions.answer: Ensuring that user data is collected and used transparently with informed consent
questions.explanation: Data privacy and transparency are core ethical responsibilities for startups. Users should understand how their data is collected, stored, and used.

questions.question: Content marketing is most effective for startups when it:
options: Primarily promotes product features and pricing
Provides genuine value and solves problems for the target audience
Focuses exclusively on the founder's personal story
Is published once and never updated
questions.answer: Provides genuine value and solves problems for the target audience
questions.explanation: Effective content marketing builds trust and attracts customers by delivering helpful, relevant content — not by simply advertising products.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The 'Key Activities' block of the Business Model Canvas refers to:" is: The activities customers perform when using the product.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The most important things a company must do to operate successfully.

questions.question: Describe the nine building blocks of the Business Model Canvas. Choose any three blocks and explain how they relate to each other for a startup of your choice.
questions.answer: The nine blocks are: Customer Segments, Value Propositions, Channels, Customer Relationships, Revenue Streams, Key Resources, Key Activities, Key Partnerships, and Cost Structure. For example, in a food delivery startup: the Customer Segment (busy urban professionals) shapes the Value Proposition (fast delivery in under 30 minutes), which in turn determines the Key Activities needed (logistics coordination, app development, driver recruitment).
questions.explanation: The canvas helps entrepreneurs visualize how all parts of a business model fit together and depend on each other.

questions.question: You have an idea for a new student tutoring app. Outline the key steps you would take — from idea to MVP — using the lean startup approach. What would your MVP look like, and how would you measure its success?
questions.answer: Steps: (1) Identify the core assumption (e.g., students will pay for on-demand peer tutoring). (2) Conduct customer discovery interviews with 10 students to validate the problem. (3) Build the simplest MVP that tests the assumption — e.g., a WhatsApp group or a simple booking form paired with in-person sessions, not a full app. (4) Measure success with specific metrics: number of sessions booked, retention rate, willingness to pay. (5) Use data to decide whether to persevere with the current model or pivot.
questions.explanation: The lean approach minimizes wasted resources by testing assumptions quickly before investing in full product development.

questions.question: What are the three main types of intellectual property protection available to startups? Give an example of each and explain why protecting IP is strategically important.
questions.answer: Three types: (1) Patent — protects inventions (e.g., a unique app algorithm); prevents competitors from copying your innovation for a set period. (2) Trademark — protects brand identity like a name or logo (e.g., a startup's logo); builds brand recognition and prevents confusion. (3) Copyright — protects original creative works (e.g., website copy, marketing videos); automatically granted upon creation. Protecting IP is strategically important because it creates defensible competitive advantages, increases company valuation, and can generate licensing revenue.
questions.explanation: IP protection transforms creative and technical innovations into business assets that can be defended and monetized.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best defines an entrepreneurial mindset?
options: Focusing only on making money quickly
Approaching problems with creativity, persistence, and a willingness to learn from failure
Avoiding any risks in business decisions
Copying successful business ideas from others
quizQuestions.answer: Approaching problems with creativity, persistence, and a willingness to learn from failure
quizQuestions.explanation: An entrepreneurial mindset involves seeing opportunities in problems, embracing calculated risk, and treating setbacks as learning experiences.

quizQuestions.question: A growth mindset, as defined by Carol Dweck, is the belief that:
options: Intelligence and talent are fixed at birth
Abilities can be developed through effort and learning
Only naturally gifted people can succeed
quizQuestions.answer: Abilities can be developed through effort and learning
quizQuestions.explanation: Carol Dweck's growth mindset research shows that people who believe abilities can grow through dedication outperform those with a fixed mindset.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following best defines an entrepreneurial mindset" is: Approaching problems with creativity, persistence, and a willingness to learn from failure.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Approaching problems with creativity, persistence, and a willingness to learn from failure is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Design thinking begins with which phase?
quizQuestions.answer: Empathize
quizQuestions.explanation: Design thinking starts with empathizing — deeply understanding the people you are designing for to uncover their real needs.

quizQuestions.question: An empathy map helps entrepreneurs:
options: Understand what customers think, feel, say, and do
Choose a legal structure for the business
quizQuestions.answer: Understand what customers think, feel, say, and do
quizQuestions.explanation: Empathy maps are visual tools that capture insights about users across four dimensions: what they Say, Think, Do, and Feel.

quizQuestions.question: A 'pain point' in the context of identifying customer problems refers to:
options: A physical injury a customer sustains
A specific problem, frustration, or unmet need that a customer experiences
The price a customer pays for a product
A complaint about poor customer service
quizQuestions.answer: A specific problem, frustration, or unmet need that a customer experiences
quizQuestions.explanation: Pain points are the problems or challenges customers face that create opportunities for entrepreneurs to deliver solutions.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Design thinking begins with which phase" is: Empathize.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Empathize is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The SCAMPER technique uses which of the following actions to generate new ideas?
options: Survey, Compare, Analyze, Measure, Plan, Execute, Review
Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Reverse
Sell, Create, Apply, Market, Price, Evaluate, Release
Start, Configure, Adjust, Manage, Promote, Evolve, Replicate
quizQuestions.answer: Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Reverse
quizQuestions.explanation: SCAMPER is an ideation checklist: each letter prompts a different way to transform an existing idea into something new.

quizQuestions.question: The key rule of effective brainstorming is:
options: Evaluate each idea immediately to save time
Only share ideas you are confident about
Generate as many ideas as possible without judging them during the session
Focus only on practical, low-cost ideas
quizQuestions.answer: Generate as many ideas as possible without judging them during the session
quizQuestions.explanation: Brainstorming works best when judgment is suspended — quantity over quality during idea generation encourages creative thinking.

quizQuestions.question: Lateral thinking, as a creative technique, involves:
options: Following established procedures step by step
Approaching problems from unexpected angles to find unconventional solutions
Using data to identify the most logical solution
Consulting experts for the correct answer
quizQuestions.answer: Approaching problems from unexpected angles to find unconventional solutions
quizQuestions.explanation: Lateral thinking (coined by Edward de Bono) challenges assumptions and looks for indirect, creative approaches rather than straightforward logical paths.

quizQuestions.question: The main goal of a customer discovery interview is to:
options: Convince the customer to buy your product
Understand the customer's actual problems and behaviors
Explain all the features of your product
quizQuestions.answer: Understand the customer's actual problems and behaviors
quizQuestions.explanation: Customer discovery is about listening and learning, not selling. You want to uncover real pain points and test your assumptions about who the customer is.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of question is most effective in a customer discovery interview?
options: 'Would you buy a product like this?'
'Don't you think this is a great idea?'
'Tell me about the last time you experienced this problem.'
'How much would you pay for this solution?'
quizQuestions.answer: 'Tell me about the last time you experienced this problem.'
quizQuestions.explanation: Open-ended behavioral questions reveal real experiences and needs; 'would you buy' questions produce unreliable hypothetical answers.

quizQuestions.question: The jobs-to-be-done (JTBD) framework focuses on:
options: The demographic profile of target customers
The functional and emotional progress a customer is trying to make in their life
The technical specifications of a product feature
The number of employment positions a startup plans to create
quizQuestions.answer: The functional and emotional progress a customer is trying to make in their life
quizQuestions.explanation: JTBD theory holds that customers 'hire' products to make progress — solve a problem or achieve a goal — rather than for product features alone.

quizQuestions.question: Validating an assumption in customer discovery means:
options: Proving that your product works technically
Confirming (or refuting) your hypothesis about a customer's problem or behavior with real evidence
Getting legal validation from a lawyer
Validating your business plan with an accountant
quizQuestions.answer: Confirming (or refuting) your hypothesis about a customer's problem or behavior with real evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: Assumption validation uses real customer data — gathered through interviews or experiments — to determine whether your beliefs about the market are accurate.

quizQuestions.question: The correct order of the lean startup cycle is:
quizQuestions.answer: Build → Measure → Learn
quizQuestions.explanation: The lean startup loop is: Build (create the MVP), Measure (collect data on user behavior), Learn (extract insights to decide what to do next).

quizQuestions.question: The purpose of an MVP is to:
options: Create a perfect product before launch
Test the most important assumption with the least effort
Compete directly with established market leaders from day one
quizQuestions.answer: Test the most important assumption with the least effort
quizQuestions.explanation: An MVP is the smallest experiment that tests your riskiest assumption, allowing you to learn quickly without over-investing in a product that may need to change.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The correct order of the lean startup cycle is:" is: Build → Measure → Learn.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Build → Measure → Learn is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The purpose of an MVP is to:" is: Create a perfect product before launch.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Test the most important assumption with the least effort.

quizQuestions.question: How many building blocks does the Business Model Canvas have?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Business Model Canvas has nine building blocks covering customers, value, infrastructure, and finances.

quizQuestions.question: The 'Value Proposition' block in the Business Model Canvas describes:
options: The bundle of products and services that create value for a specific customer segment
The marketing channels you use to reach customers
The costs required to operate the business
quizQuestions.answer: The bundle of products and services that create value for a specific customer segment
quizQuestions.explanation: The Value Proposition is the reason customers choose you — it describes the specific value you deliver to solve their problems or fulfill their needs.

quizQuestions.question: Revenue Streams in the Business Model Canvas represent:
options: The cash a company generates from each customer segment
The channels used to deliver the product
The key partners that support the business
quizQuestions.answer: The cash a company generates from each customer segment
quizQuestions.explanation: Revenue Streams describe how the company earns money from its value propositions — through sales, subscriptions, licensing, advertising, etc.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "How many building blocks does the Business Model Canvas have" is: 9.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. 9 is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is the difference between primary and secondary market research?
options: Primary research is more expensive; secondary research is free
Primary research collects new data directly; secondary research uses existing data
Primary research is conducted by large companies; secondary by startups
Primary research uses quantitative methods only; secondary uses qualitative methods only
quizQuestions.answer: Primary research collects new data directly; secondary research uses existing data
quizQuestions.explanation: Primary research (surveys, interviews, experiments) generates new data for your specific question. Secondary research uses existing data (reports, studies) already published.

quizQuestions.question: A competitive analysis helps entrepreneurs:
options: Understand the competitive landscape and identify how to differentiate
Eliminate all competitors from the market
Determine the salary structure for employees
quizQuestions.answer: Understand the competitive landscape and identify how to differentiate
quizQuestions.explanation: Competitive analysis reveals who else is solving a similar problem, how they do it, and where gaps exist — informing differentiation strategy.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The difference between primary and secondary market research is "Primary research collects new data directly; secondary research uses existing data".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Primary research collects new data directly; secondary research uses existing data is correct.

quizQuestions.question: A value proposition primarily answers the question:
options: How much does the product cost?
Why should a customer choose your product over alternatives?
How many employees does the company have?
quizQuestions.answer: Why should a customer choose your product over alternatives?
quizQuestions.explanation: A value proposition communicates the specific benefit you deliver and why it is the best option for your target customer.

quizQuestions.question: USP stands for:
quizQuestions.answer: Unique Selling Point
quizQuestions.explanation: A Unique Selling Point (USP) is the specific feature or benefit that distinguishes a product from all competitors.

quizQuestions.question: Positioning in marketing refers to:
options: The physical location of your business
How a product is perceived in the minds of target customers relative to competitors
The position a CEO holds in a company
The placement of products on a store shelf
quizQuestions.answer: How a product is perceived in the minds of target customers relative to competitors
quizQuestions.explanation: Positioning shapes how customers think about your product — it is about the space your brand occupies in the customer's mind relative to alternatives.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A value proposition primarily answers the question:" is: Why should a customer choose your product over alternatives?.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Why should a customer choose your product over alternatives? is correct.
